name: Jordan Taylor
occupation: College senior, Environmental Studies major, part-time barista
backstory: Jordan is thoughtful and genuinely cares about sustainability — it's their major. But there's a disconnect between their values and their behavior. They spend 3+ hours daily on TikTok and Instagram, and viral product recommendations feel like discovery, not advertising. 'TikTok made me buy it' is said without irony. They've bought a sunrise alarm clock, three skincare routines, a Stanley cup, and a set of aesthetic desk accessories in the last two months — all from creator recommendations. Their dorm room looks like a curated content set. They haven't developed a strong internal compass for what they actually want vs. what's trending. They volunteer at an animal shelter on weekends, which they love.
emotional_triggers: Viral product videos with high production value and social proof
Peer group trends — everyone in their friend group buys the same things
'Aesthetic' culture — the pressure to have a visually cohesive life
Fear of being out of touch or behind trends
The seamless shopping experience embedded in social media (tap to buy)
existing_strengths: Genuinely curious and open to new perspectives
Values sustainability (even if behavior doesn't always match)
Loves learning — reads widely beyond coursework
Volunteers at an animal shelter — has strong empathy for living things
Good sense of humor and self-deprecating awareness ('I know I'm a walking TikTok ad')
current_situation: Jordan just watched a TikTok about a $45 'life-changing' water bottle with a motivational time tracker on the side. They already have two perfectly good water bottles. Three friends have posted about the same bottle this week. They're in the library supposedly studying for finals but have the product page open on their phone. They feel silly for wanting it but also feel like they're missing out.
what_success_looks_like: Jordan laughs at the absurdity, puts the phone down, recognizes the pattern of manufactured desire, and maybe texts a friend about it instead of buying it. Long-term: starts noticing the 'TikTok made me buy it' pattern as it happens.
